6. J. P. Gabathuler and F. Weinberg: Metall. Trans. B, 1983, vol. 14B, pp. 733-41. # Low Temperature Mechanical Behavior of Microalloyed and Controlled-Rolled Fe-Mn-Al-C-X Alloys YOUNG G. KIM, YEE S. PARK, and JAE K. HAN This work is concerned with the development of high strength–high toughness cryogenic Fe-Mn-Al-C-X alloys containing 0.1 wt pct Nb or 0.1 wt pct V as microalloying elements, and which are processed by a controlled hot rolling process. The base alloy composition was Fe-30Mn-5Al-0.3C (alloy A), $^{1}$ to which 0.1 wt pct Nb (alloy B) and 0.1 wt pct V (alloy C) were added. The alloys were vacuum induction melted and were subjected to controlled hot rolling after soaking at 1250 °C for 2 hours. The final reduction ratio was 25 pct and the finishing temperature was 800 °C. Figure 1 shows results of tensile test at RT, $-40^{\circ}$ , $-100^{\circ}$ , and $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and also includes data reported by Charles et al. for the cold rolled and recrystallized base alloy. As shown in Figure 1, the yield stress of alloy A at $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ increased from $600\mathrm{MPa}$ in the recrystallized condition to $855\mathrm{MPa}$ by applying the controlled hot rolling process. This remarkable increase in strength is due primarily to the refinement of grain size and dislocation substructure following the controlled rolling process. The ASTM grain size was 11 for the controlled hot rolling compared with ASTM 4 for the recrystallized condition. Niobium was found to be a more potent strengthening microalloying element compared to vanadium. At $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the yield stress of alloy B (0.1 Nb) was 944 MPa, compared with 907 MPa for alloy C (0.1 V). The yield and tensile strengths of alloys B and C were equal to those of the 9 wt pct Ni cryogenic steel, while the impact energy of the alloy B was twice that of the 9 wt pct Ni cryogenic steel, $^{2}$ as shown in Figure 2. An interesting observation in this study was the increase in elongation with decreasing temperature, as shown in Figure 3. For example, alloy B had 29 pct strain at RT, compared to 57 pct at $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . This inverse ductility behavior is ideal for cryogenic material requirements. The reversing behavior was also observed in the recrystallized Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy. $^{1}$ In general, alloys A, B, and C possessed very high elongations. Figure 4 shows the configuration of tensile tested samples before and after testing at RT, $-40^{\circ}$ , $-100^{\circ}$ , and $-196^{\circ}$ C A, B, and C. for alloy B. They clearly show the increasing elongation with decreasing test temperature. However, the reduction in area did not change greatly with variation in test temperature, as shown in Figure 3 for alloy B. Engineering stress-strain curves of alloy B at various test temperatures are shown in Figure 5. It shows that the maximum elongation occurred at $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the necking occurred earlier as the test temperature increased. ![](images/f65d3afdcb7f28f3c366f6f1b7e4c47df7b068b370958fd06a6b4a3f5b819119.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a black rectangular electronic component with multiple oval cutouts, placed next to a ruler for scale (no text or symbols on the component itself) </details> Fig. 4—Configuration of tensile specimens tested at $-196^{\circ}$ , $-100^{\circ}$ , $-40^{\circ}$ , and $25^{\circ}C$ (RT) for alloy B. ![](images/ed909dcab58b00d867fd04bc1f0b0f84dada6c395b8d2d3329c80489cc2c8d6f.jpg) <details> <summary>line</summary> | STRAIN (%) | STRESS (MPa) | Temperature | | ---------- | ------------ | ----------- | | ~5         | ~950         | -196°C      | | ~30        | ~800         | -100°C      | | ~30        | ~750         | -40°C       | | ~30        | ~500         | R.T.        | </details> Fig. 5—Engineering stress-strain curves of alloy B tested at $-196^{\circ}$ , $-100^{\circ}$ , $-40^{\circ}$ , and $25^{\circ}C$ . It appears that the increasing elongation with decreasing temperature is due to TRIP behavior. $^{3}$ In order to attempt to detect the formation of strain-induced phases during deformation, alloy B was strained by different amounts (0, 20, 50 pct) at $-196\;^{\circ}C$ . The samples were heat treated at $850\;^{\circ}C$ for 28 hours before applying the strains to produce coarse grains. Figure 6 shows optical microstructures of the deformed samples at $-196\;^{\circ}C$ , where the amount of strain-induced phase is clearly seen to increase with increasing strain. In order to characterize the strain-induced phase, tensile samples were subjected to X-ray diffraction before and after testing at RT and $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , using Ni-filtered $\mathrm{Cu - K_{\alpha}}$ radiation. Figure 7 shows X-ray diffraction patterns of tensile tested samples. Curve 1 is for the sample prior to tensile test, and curves 2 and 3 are for samples tested at RT and $-196^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The X-ray diffraction traces showed diffraction maxima corresponding to an fcc phase only and no bcc or hcp martensite reflections were observed. ![](images/f8cb21b43399a98e2e66fe5dbde4550c44c456addf623e7cf75fb06d6d198d1c.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material's grain structure with visible cracks and texture (scale bar: 50 μm) </details> (a) ![](images/3b3eac8ab566dcb905d49a27ddaa9797b58db3b3f6cdbe14c6fe78fffad34977.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material's grain structure with 50 μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond scale indicator) </details> (b) ![](images/d43a2621fcf8cfc1ced3656a1222efdc73ff72507ae81d04c8e10bfb6361808d.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material's grain structure with visible inclusions and a 50 μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond scale) </details> (c) Fig. 6—Optical micrographs of alloy B as a function of applied strain at $-196\ ^{\circ}C$ (etched with 2 pct nital): (a) before test, (b) 20 pct strain, and (c) 50 pct strain. ![](images/bf097fe57f820246d6252a484062ccbdef7011bc7bb3d90126d40197ad8d43c0.jpg) <details> <summary>line</summary> | Sample | Peak Label             | X-Degree (2θ) | |--------|------------------------|---------------| | as rolled |                     |                       | | tensile tested at R.T. | γ(220)               |                       | | tensile tested at R.T. | γ(200)               |                       | | tensile tested at R.T. | γ(111)               |                       | | tensile tested at -196°C |                     |                       | </details> Fig. 7—X-ray diffraction patterns of alloy B before and after tensile tests at RT and $-196^{\circ}C$ . In order to characterize the nature of the strain-induced phase, the sample tensile tested at $-196\ ^{\circ}C$ was further examined by SEM and TEM. Figure 8 is a scanning electron micrograph of the sample strained 50 pct at $-196\ ^{\circ}C$ of which the optical microstructure is shown in Figure 6. Figures 9(a) and 9(b) are the bright- and dark-field TEM images of these foils of that sample, tensile tested at $-196\ ^{\circ}C$ . The electron diffraction pattern and its analysis are shown in Figures 9(c) and 9(d), respectively. The analysis showed that the strain-induced “phase” was a consequence of mechanical twinning. It is known that the strain- or stress-induced phases in TRIP steels are martensites. $^{3,4,5}$ However, in this work, the strain-induced “phase” was confined to be mechanical twinning in steel. Formation of mechanical twins is possible in high manganese alloys since manganese is known to significantly lower the stacking fault energy. $^{6}$ It is believed that the restriction of slip by the formation of stacking faults and the low temperature environment favored the formation of mechanical twins in this alloy. The behavior of increasing elongation with decreasing temperature is attributed to be due to strain-induced mechanical twinning. Large uniform elongations can be achieved since local deformation can be prevented. If a local deformation, neck, starts to develop in the middle of gage length (Figure 10(a)), strain-induced mechanical twins would preferentially form in this region. Further deformation would be prevented by hardening due to the mechanical twins. Necking could then start at other regions (Figure 10(b)), but would similarly again be prevented. The resultant multiple necking mechanism (Figure 10(c)) yields the high uniform elongations (10(d)). At RT, the amount of mechanical twins formed is not enough to prevent local deformation and as the temperature is lowered, formation of mechanical twins becomes more favorable. It is proposed that the increasing elongation with decreasing temperature is associated with a favorable temperature sensitivity of the transformation kinetics of mechanical twinning in these niobium or vanadium modified alloys. ![](images/eae12a2aa604fb7d47a4395a691d7ad1c69b80ad2c8ce53e42c68d0281b2d3dc.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material's microstructure 6. I. Tamura: J. Iron and Steel Inst. Jpn., 1979, vol. 56, p. 61. # Discussion of “Enhanced Tensile Strength for Electrodeposited Nickel-Copper Multilayer Composites”\* # CHARLES A. PARKER In a recent paper $^{1}$ Tench and White reported on the tensile strength of electrodeposited layered composites. They alternated layers of pure Cu with Ni-2 wt pct Cu where the pure Cu layers were approximately $\frac{1}{9}$ the thickness of the Ni-2 pct Cu layers. Specimens were tensile tested with the tensile axis parallel to the layer plane. Tench and White found that as the thickness of the layers decreased, the tensile strength of the composite was approx- ![](images/d07d90698034e938fa4626b9712798c149067ea5cac1efee536740d0d98bbcb6.jpg) <details> <summary>scatter</summary> | RUN | d^-1/2 (μm^-1/2) | ULTIMATE TENSILE STRENGTH (MPa) | | --- | --- | --- | | RUN 1 | 0.5 | 600 | | RUN 1 | 0.8 | 620 | | RUN 1 | 1.0 | 610 | | RUN 1 | 1.2 | 580 | | RUN 1 | 1.5 | 550 | | RUN 1 | 2.0 | 630 | | RUN 1 | 3.0 | 700 | | RUN 1 | 4.0 | 800 | | RUN 1 | 5.0 | 900 | | RUN 1 | 6.0 | 1050 | | RUN 1 | 7.0 | 1100 | | RUN 1 | 8.0 | 1250 | | RUN 1 | 9.0 | 1350 | | RUN 1 | 10.0 | 1400 | | RUN 1, CONSTANT CURRENT | 1.0 | 620 | | RUN 1, CONSTANT CURRENT | 3.5 | 980 | | RUN 2 | 1.5 | 580 | | RUN 2 | 2.5 | 850 | | RUN 2 | 4.5 | 1080 | | RUN 2 | 5.5 | 1380 | | RUN 2 | 6.5 | 1180 | | RUN 2 | 9.5 | 1220 | </details> Fig. 1 — Data of Ref. 1, replotted to show the Hall-Petch relationship. The copper layer thickness is d, while $\overline{d}$ is the average thickness. Data point descriptions from Ref. 1. imately constant until the copper layer thickness reached about 0.4 $\mu$ m. For thinner layers, the tensile strength rose dramatically. The strength vs layer thickness can be explained simply as a grain size effect. Hall $^{2}$ and Petch $^{3}$ showed for polycrystalline alloys that the yield strength, $\sigma_{y}$ , is proportional to $D^{-1/2}$ , where D is the grain diameter. As grain size decreases, the stress concentrations where slip bands intersect grain boundaries decrease, and propagation of slip from one grain to another becomes more difficult. The data of Tench and White have been replotted to show the Hall-Petch relationship (Figure 1). They used two different thicknesses in each sample; scales are shown for the thinner layer and average layer thickness. Although there is considerable scatter in the data, tensile strength is proportional to $d^{-1/2}$ , where d is the layer thickness. The Hall-Petch relation is strictly valid for yield strength only; however, when ductility is low, as in the present case, ultimate tensile strength should follow the relationship as well.
1. Dennis Tench and John White: Metall. Trans. A, 1984, vol. 15A, pp. 2039-40.
2. E. O. Hall: Proc. Phys. Soc. (London), 1951, vol. 64B, pp. 747-53.
3. N.J. Petch: J. Iron Steel Inst., 1953, vol. 174, pp. 25-28.
